summary.brief: Smith v. Zama. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: On November 20, 2018, plaintiff Lucas Smith (hereinafter Smith) presented to defendant Arnot Ogden Medical Center (hereinafter AOMC) with various complaints, including intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A CT pulmonary angiogram revealed a massive 9.8-centimeter ascending aortic aneurysm, and additional testing led to a diagnosis of atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Defendant Nche Zama, a cardiothoracic surgeon, performed the surgical repair of Smith's ascending aortic aneurysm on November 26, 2018.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On November 30, 2018, he was discharged by McField under the care of Zama with certain medications, including an antiarrhythmic (Amiodarone 200 mg) and an antiplatelet (Aspirin 81 mg).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Smith returned to AOMC on December 2, 2018, experiencing shortness of breath, increased abdominal pain and distention, and diarrhea.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Grossi opined that defendants appropriately converted Smith from atrial fibrillation to a normal sinus rhythm by virtue of the successful aneurysm repair.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendant's expert

evidence_index.text: The expert further explained that anticoagulants given to patients with intermittent atrial fibrillation can decrease the risk of embolic events post-cardiac surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Supreme Court granted defendants' motions for summary judgment and dismissed the complaint.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, when giving plaintiffs the benefit of all reasonable inferences as the nonmoving parties, Supreme Court should have denied defendants' motions for summary judgment.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: ORDERED that the order is reversed, on the law, with costs, and motions denied.
evidence_index.speaker: Court order

initial_state.chief_complaint: Intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath. Initial diagnostic testing revealed a rapid, irregular heart rate and high blood pressure.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Massive ascending aortic aneurysm with atrial fibrillation

requestables.name: CT Pulmonary Angiogram
requestables.description: CT scan to evaluate the pulmonary arteries and aorta

reveal.decision_impact: Prompted surgical repair of the aneurysm

requestables.name: INR
requestables.description: International Normalized Ratio to assess anticoagulation status

reveal.clinical_significance: Considered inadequately anticoagulated by plaintiff's expert
reveal.decision_impact: Contributed to decision not to prescribe anticoagulants

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to AOMC with chest pain and shortness of breath

timeline_phases.name: Postoperative Care
timeline_phases.description: Post-surgical management after aortic aneurysm repair

key_events.event: Patient discharged with Amiodarone and Aspirin
Patient readmitted with shortness of breath and abdominal pain

decision_points.clinical_context: Postoperative management after aortic aneurysm repair
decision_points.prompt: What medications should be prescribed at discharge?

options.clinical_reasoning: To manage atrial fibrillation risk and prevent embolic events
Believed to be sufficient for anticoagulation and rhythm control

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant prescribed Amiodarone and Aspirin only
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed aspirin was adequate for anticoagulation

expected_action_court.description: Beta blockers and anticoagulants should have been prescribed
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated increased risk of embolic events without anticoagulation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to prescribe adequate anticoagulation led to embolic events
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Beta blockers and anticoagulants should have been prescribed to prevent embolic complications
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed ischemic bowel requiring extensive resection
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Significant bowel resection and associated complications

key_findings: Defendants' care was not a departure from the accepted standard of care

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Adequate anticoagulation could have prevented embolic events

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to AOMC with chest pain and shortness of breath
Surgical repair of ascending aortic aneurysm performed
Patient discharged with Amiodarone and Aspirin

diagnoses.diagnosis: Ischemic bowel secondary to embolic event

treatments.treatment: Surgical repair of ascending aortic aneurysm

allegations.allegation: Failure to prescribe beta blockers and anticoagulants

court_findings.finding: Defendants' care was not a departure from the accepted standard of care
